main_summary.summary: Dr. Doris Derby, interviewed by Joe Mosnier on the campus of Georgia State University for the Civil Rights History Project, describes a path from a Williamsbridge, Bronx childhood through SNCC field organizing in Albany, Georgia (summer 1962) and Jackson, Mississippi (1963 onward). She traces family influences: maternal grandparents in Bangor, Maine (charter NAACP members); her great-aunt Jesse, a missionary in Monrovia, Liberia; and her aunt Julia Green, W.E.B. Du Bois's executive secretary later recruited to integrate the federal secretarial workforce in Washington. Derby studied dance at the Katherine Dunham school, used the Schomburg Library as a teenager, joined the NAACP at sixteen, and traveled to Nigeria on a 1960 NAACP scholarship before the country's independence. Through Hunter College's Christian Human Relations Club and AMSAC, she connected with the Northern Student Movement. After meeting Bob Zellner, Ruby Doris Smith, James Forman, and Charles Sherrod in Atlanta's SNCC office, she joined the Albany Movement, then accepted Bob Moses's invitation to a Mississippi adult-literacy pilot project tied to voter registration. In Jackson she co-founded the Free Southern Theatre with John O'Neal and Gilbert Moses, worked through COFO, taught Head Start outside Holly Springs and in Durant, helped build the Poor People's Corporation and Liberty House handcraft co-operatives, and documented the movement through Southern Media photography from 1968 onward.
key_themes: SNCC field organizing in Albany and Mississippi
Poor People's Corporation and Liberty House co-operatives
Pan-African cultural identification through Schomburg, AMSAC, and Nigeria travel
main_summary.historical_significance: Derby's testimony documents the cultural-arts arm of SNCC organizing in Mississippi from 1963 forward, an aspect of the movement less covered than direct-action campaigns. She provides first-person account of the Free Southern Theatre's founding rationale, the operational mechanics of COFO and the Poor People's Corporation handcraft co-operatives, and the recruitment, training, and facility logistics of early Head Start centers around Holly Springs and Durant. Her recollection of family precedents — a grandmother and uncle as charter NAACP members in 1920s Bangor, an aunt working for W.E.B. Du Bois, a great-aunt missionary in Liberia — establishes a multi-generational Black intellectual and activist lineage rooted outside the South.

chapters.title: Family Roots in Bangor and the Bronx
chapters.summary: Derby describes her maternal grandparents in Bangor, Maine — her grandmother an original charter member of an NAACP chapter founded in the 1920s, her grandfather a Bangor businessman — and her paternal grandparents, including her father's experience as the only African-American in his civil engineering class at the University of Pennsylvania and his refusal to enter through a service elevator to collect his awards.

chapters.title: Quest for Black Cultural Images
chapters.summary: Derby recounts seeking positive Black images as a child — finding them in great-aunt Jesse's letters from missionary work in Monrovia, Liberia; in dance lessons at the Katherine Dunham school; and in research at the Schomburg Library in Manhattan. She describes her father's family photography practice as a precedent for her own later documentary work.
related_events: Katherine Dunham Dance school scholarship audition

chapters.title: NAACP Youth and Family Discussions
chapters.summary: Derby describes joining her church's NAACP youth chapter and the dinner-table political conversations in her extended family — her father's University of Pennsylvania story, her uncle's dental training at Tufts and his eventual work as a sky cap, and her aunt Julia Green's career as W.E.B. Du Bois's executive secretary in Boston before federal recruitment to integrate the Washington DC secretarial workforce in the 1940s.

chapters.title: Hunter College, Nigeria, and AMSAC
chapters.summary: Derby describes summer travel to a Navajo reservation in New Mexico, then a 1960 NAACP scholarship trip to Nigeria the summer before independence, with stops in Paris and Rome. She traces her involvement with Hunter College's Anthropology Club, the African Scholarship Committee supporting John F. Kennedy airlift students, and AMSAC's African-American cultural connections work, plus a Hunter visit by Malcolm X.

chapters.title: Summer 1962 in Albany, Georgia
chapters.summary: Derby describes a planned summer 1962 trip to Mexico for anthropology study diverted into SNCC work when she stopped first in Atlanta. She met James Forman, Bob Zellner, and Ruby Doris Smith at the SNCC office, then traveled with Forman and Zellner to Albany, Georgia. She canvassed for voter registration with the Albany Movement and was eventually asked by Charles Sherrod to return to New York to raise funds and goods.

chapters.title: New York SNCC Office and March on Washington
chapters.summary: Derby describes returning to New York to teach school by day and run SNCC fundraising by evening through 1962–63. She partnered with Rae Brandstein on a school-bus food, clothing, and book drive for Mississippi; helped establish the New York SNCC office; co-founded New York Artists for SNCC; and joined the recruiting committee for the March on Washington under Bayard Rustin.

chapters.title: Bob Moses and the Move to Mississippi
chapters.summary: Derby recounts Bob Moses traveling to her New York fundraising event, then repeatedly calling to recruit her to a Mississippi adult-literacy pilot project tied to SNCC voter registration. She initially declined, planning graduate study in Mexico, but the spring 1963 Birmingham violence — police dogs and fire hoses on children — prompted her to commit to Mississippi after the March on Washington.

chapters.title: Jackson, COFO, and Field Work
chapters.summary: Derby describes arriving in Jackson in fall 1963 as a SNCC field secretary on $10-a-week payroll, housed with local families, working out of the Council of Federated Organizations umbrella office. She describes COFO's coordination of SNCC, CORE, and NAACP staff on voter registration, demonstrations, integration of facilities, and the mock gubernatorial election, alongside the dangers of integrated cars, the limits of federal protection, and the spiritual strength of community members.

chapters.title: Founding the Free Southern Theatre
chapters.summary: Derby describes co-founding the Free Southern Theatre in October 1963 with John O'Neal and Gilbert Moses while housed on the Tougaloo College campus. The group conceived theatre as a vehicle for the other arts and a way to take plays into rural Mississippi — churches, cotton fields, grocery stores. Bill Hutchinson, a Tougaloo theatre professor, joined the effort. The first production was Purlie Victorious in spring 1964; the theatre lasted 19 years, eventually moving to Tulane University in New Orleans.

chapters.title: Head Start in Mississippi
chapters.summary: Derby describes joining Mississippi Head Start as resource teacher at a center outside Holly Springs after a University of Michigan research project at Tougaloo, Talladega, and Tuskegee. She describes recruiting teachers from mothers and community members because regular Black teachers feared association with the program, making toys from recycled materials, recording children's responses to make custom books, and later serving as troubleshooter trainer at the Durant child development center — a building constructed by the local Black community on donated land.

chapters.title: Poor People's Corporation and Liberty House
chapters.summary: Derby describes her work with the Poor People's Corporation as an umbrella for handcraft co-operatives across Mississippi, Louisiana, and Georgia, with Liberty House in Jackson handling raw-materials purchasing, storage, and marketing. She traveled to potential co-op groups setting minimum requirements of ten members and seed money, recruited Northern volunteer crafts teachers, learned candle-making and leather work to train co-ops in turn, and singles out the Una sewing co-operative's black rag dolls as a notable product.

chapters.title: Southern Media and the Photographic Record
chapters.summary: Derby describes joining Southern Media in 1968 — founded in 1967 by two white organizers (Bill Pell and a Midwestern colleague) to train young Black Mississippians in documentary film and photography. She describes documenting women in all walks of movement work, children, men engaged in constructive activity, the flourishing handcraft and farmers co-operatives, the Tufts–Mound Bayou healthcare partnership, and her later work with the Atlanta-based Sistography Black Women's Photography Collective.

chapters.title: Black Womanism and Closing Reflections
chapters.summary: Derby describes her self-identification as a Black womanist drawing on Alice Walker's framing — a Black woman working alongside family and community to develop the community and bring Black people forward, with all ages included and political and cultural discussion not hidden from children. She links this philosophy to her family's dinner-table conversations and her continuing work starting a theatre group and an African-American filmmakers group at Georgia State.

loc_video.caption: Doris Adelaide Derby oral history interview conducted by Joseph Mosnier in Atlanta, Georgia, 2011 April 26
